frameworks.name: metacognitive efficiency
frameworks.one_line: behavioural measure of confidence tracking first-order task accuracy across domains

frameworks.name: domain-general vs domain-specific metacognition
frameworks.one_line: competing accounts of whether one mechanism supports metacognition across tasks

frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: framework distinguishing 2AFC and yes/no judgement types underlying first-order tasks

core_question: Does cross-domain correlation in metacognitive efficiency between perception and memory depend on the first-order task type used?
key_assumptions: metacognitive efficiency is comparable across perception and memory domains
2AFC and yes/no judgements probe different first-order computations
behavioural correlations index shared metacognitive mechanism
manipulations: first-order task type (2AFC vs yes/no) across visual and memory tasks
three experimental configurations mirroring prior conflicting studies
matched sample size across experiments for statistical power
key_findings: replicated positive cross-domain correlation when both tasks used 2AFC
replicated absence of correlation when tasks mixed 2AFC and yes/no
no significant cross-domain correlation when both tasks used yes/no
interpretations: prior cross-domain correlation was not merely an artefact of identical task format
absence of correlation in mixed-task studies may stem from yes/no judgement use
